text:
COMPREHENSIVE SOLID TUMOR NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT

Molecular Oncology Laboratory — Certified Genomics Division

---

PATIENT INFORMATION
Patient Name: Marcus T. Delgado
Date of Birth: 1965
Sex: Male
MRN: [REDACTED]
Ordering Physician: Medical Oncology / Gastrointestinal Oncology Service
Diagnosis: Adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon, Stage IV (cT3 N2b M1b), with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen ID: [LAB-ACCESSION REDACTED]
Specimen Type: Formalin-Fixed Paraffin-Embedded (FFPE) tissue
Anatomic Site: Sigmoid colon — primary tumor
Procedure Type: Colonoscopic biopsy
Material Received: Three paraffin-embedded tissue cores; H&E-reviewed for tumor content prior to nucleic acid extraction
Tumor Cellularity (H&E-estimated): Approximately 65% tumor nuclei
DNA Quality: Sufficient for analysis; DIN score 3.8 (acceptable for FFPE NGS)
RNA Quality: Sufficient for fusion analysis

PANEL DESCRIPTION
Panel: Comprehensive Solid Tumor NGS — 523-gene panel
Analysis Includes: Somatic single nucleotide variants (SNVs), small insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations (CNAs), gene fusions/rearrangements, microsatellite instability (MSI), tumor mutational burden (TMB)
Germline Filtering: Paired germline analysis performed using peripheral blood leukocyte DNA; somatic vs. germline distinction applied
Sequencing Platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Mean Coverage Depth: 872x (target regions); minimum 250x
Analytic Sensitivity: ≥5% VAF for SNVs/indels in adequately covered regions
Referenced Genome: GRCh38/hg38
Classification Framework: ESMO/ASCO/AMP/CAP tiering (Tier I–IV)

---

SECTION 1: CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS (TIER I / TIER II)

1.1 KRAS p.G12D — TIER I (Oncogenic Driver, Therapeutic Relevance)
  Gene: KRAS (Kirsten rat sarcoma viral proto-oncogene)
  Variant: p.Gly12Asp (G12D)
  Nucleotide Change: c.35G>A
  Exon: 2 (codon 12)
  Variant Classification: Missense, activating
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
  Coverage at locus: 1,047 reads; 440 variant reads
  Interpretation: KRAS p.G12D is one of the most common activating mutations in colorectal adenocarcinoma, occurring in approximately 13–14% of all CRC cases. This substitution impairs intrinsic GTPase activity, locking KRAS in the active GTP-bound state and constitutively activating downstream RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling pathways. This mutation is an established predictive biomarker of resistance to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab); such therapies are contraindicated in any tumor harboring mutations in KRAS codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, or 146 (extended RAS panel). KRAS p.G12D is under active investigation as a direct therapeutic target (e.g., MRTX1133 and related covalent KRASG12D inhibitors in clinical trials); however, no approved KRAS G12D-specific targeted therapy is currently available in the standard-of-care setting.
  Clinical Implication: CONTRAINDICATION to anti-EGFR therapy; eligibility for KRAS G12D-directed clinical trials should be explored.

---

SECTION 2: ADDITIONAL SOMATIC MUTATIONS (TIER I / TIER II / TIER III)

2.1 APC p.R1450* — TIER I (Oncogenic)
  Gene: APC (adenomatous polyposis coli, tumor suppressor)
  Variant: p.Arg1450* (R1450*)
  Nucleotide Change: c.4348C>T
  Exon: 15 (mutation cluster region, MCR)
  Variant Classification: Nonsense (stop-gain), loss-of-function
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
  Interpretation: APC is the gatekeeper tumor suppressor gene in colorectal carcinogenesis, mutated in >80% of sporadic CRC cases. Truncating mutations within the MCR (codons 1286–1513) result in loss of β-catenin regulatory function, leading to constitutive WNT/β-catenin transcriptional activation. The high VAF (48%) and nonsense classification are consistent with biallelic loss of function (second allele likely lost via LOH at chromosome 5q, consistent with the overall CNA pattern). This is an early clonal driver event. No specific APC-directed therapeutic exists; this finding is of diagnostic/biologic significance and is consistent with sporadic (non-polyposis) colorectal adenocarcinoma.

2.2 TP53 p.R248W — TIER I (Oncogenic)
  Gene: TP53 (tumor protein p53, tumor suppressor)
  Variant: p.Arg248Trp (R248W)
  Nucleotide Change: c.742C>T
  Exon: 7 (DNA-binding domain)
  Variant Classification: Missense, dominant-negative/gain-of-function
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 45%
  Interpretation: TP53 p.R248W is among the most frequently occurring TP53 mutations in human cancer, affecting a critical arginine residue that directly contacts DNA in the major groove. This hotspot mutation confers dominant-negative activity (the mutant protein can oligomerize with wild-type p53 to suppress its tumor suppressive function) and gain-of-function oncogenic properties (promotion of cell migration, invasion, and chemoresistance). The VAF of 45% is consistent with clonal, likely early-onset somatic event present in the dominant tumor cell population. This finding is expected in colorectal adenocarcinoma and does not confer eligibility for any current standard-of-care targeted agents; APR-246 (eprenetapopt) and related p53 reactivators remain investigational.

2.3 SMAD4 p.R361H — TIER II (Oncogenic, Clinically Relevant)
  Gene: SMAD4 (SMAD family member 4 / DPC4, tumor suppressor)
  Variant: p.Arg361H (R361H)
  Nucleotide Change: c.1082G>A
  Exon: 8 (MH2 domain)
  Variant Classification: Missense, likely loss-of-function
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
  Supporting CNA: Chromosome 18q loss (see Section 3) involving the SMAD4 locus, consistent with biallelic inactivation
  Interpretation: SMAD4 (DPC4) encodes a central mediator of TGF-β/BMP signaling. Loss-of-function mutations occur in approximately 10–20% of colorectal cancers and are enriched in patients with distant metastatic disease. SMAD4 loss is associated with a more aggressive clinical phenotype, including higher rates of distant metastasis and worse prognosis. Importantly, SMAD4 loss in the context of colorectal cancer has been associated with resistance to anti-EGFR therapy (independent of RAS mutation status) and may predict for inferior outcomes with standard chemotherapy regimens, though evidence is evolving. The relatively lower VAF (31%) compared to APC and TP53 is consistent with either subclonal accumulation or partial LOH at the 18q locus. This finding is of established prognostic significance and should be noted in the context of therapeutic decision-making.
  Note: SMAD4 co-mutation with PIK3CA (see 2.4) is observed in this tumor; this combination has been associated with particularly poor outcomes in metastatic CRC.

2.4 PIK3CA p.E545K — TIER II (Oncogenic, Clinically Relevant)
  Gene: PIK3CA (phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha)
  Variant: p.Glu545Lys (E545K)
  Nucleotide Change: c.1633G>A
  Exon: 9 (helical domain, hotspot)
  Variant Classification: Missense, activating
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 19%
  Interpretation: PIK3CA encodes the catalytic p110α subunit of class I PI3K, a key node in the PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling axis. The E545K mutation is a recurrent activating hotspot, resulting in enhanced PI3K enzymatic activity and downstream AKT activation independent of upstream RTK signaling. In colorectal cancer, PIK3CA mutations occur in approximately 15–20% of cases; exon 9 mutations (such as E545K) are associated with constitutive PI3K pathway activation but may confer differential sensitivity to PI3K/AKT inhibitors compared to exon 20 mutations. The subclonal VAF (19%) suggests this mutation may have arisen later in clonal evolution or in a subclone. No approved PIK3CA-specific inhibitor is available for CRC; alpelisib is approved in PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer and could be considered in a clinical trial context. The combination of PIK3CA mutation with KRAS G12D and SMAD4 loss creates a highly activated oncogenic signaling milieu with multiple converging pathway activations.

2.5 FBXW7 p.R465H — TIER III (Likely Oncogenic)
  Gene: FBXW7 (F-box and WD repeat domain-containing 7, tumor suppressor)
  Variant: p.Arg465His (R465H)
  Nucleotide Change: c.1394G>A
  Exon: 9 (WD40 repeat domain, substrate-recognition domain)
  Variant Classification: Missense, likely loss-of-function
  Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 14%
  Interpretation: FBXW7 encodes the substrate-recognition component of an SCF-type E3 ubiquitin ligase complex responsible for the ubiquitylation and proteasomal degradation of key oncoproteins including cyclin E1, c-MYC, c-JUN, NOTCH, and mTOR. Mutations in the WD40 repeat domain (including at hotspot arginine residues R465, R479, R505) impair substrate binding and lead to accumulation of these oncoproteins. The subclonal VAF (14%) suggests a later clonal event. FBXW7 mutations may contribute to resistance to certain chemotherapeutic agents. No approved targeted therapy directly exploits FBXW7 loss; however, FBXW7 mutation can result in substrate accumulation (e.g., cyclin E1 overexpression) that may confer vulnerability to CDK2 inhibitors (investigational).

---

SECTION 3: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS

3.1 MYC Amplification — TIER II (Oncogenic)
  Gene: MYC (v-myc avian myelocytomatosis viral oncogene homolog)
  Locus: 8q24.21
  Copy Number: 6.8-fold relative gain (estimated gene copy number ~14 copies)
  Interpretation: MYC amplification occurs in approximately 5–10% of colorectal cancers and is associated with aggressive tumor biology and high proliferative index. MYC functions as a master transcriptional regulator driving expression of hundreds of genes involved in cell cycle progression, ribosome biogenesis, and metabolic reprogramming. MYC amplification in the context of FBXW7 mutation (see 2.5) is particularly notable, as FBXW7 normally targets c-MYC for proteasomal degradation; the combination of MYC amplification and FBXW7-mediated reduction in MYC degradation creates synergistic MYC pathway overactivation. Direct MYC inhibition remains investigational.

3.2 CDK8 Gain — TIER III (Potentially Oncogenic)
  Gene: CDK8 (cyclin-dependent kinase 8)
  Locus: 13q12.13
  Copy Number: Low-level gain (estimated 3–4 copies)
  Interpretation: CDK8 is an oncogene amplified and overexpressed in approximately 25–30% of colorectal cancers. CDK8 encodes a kinase component of the Mediator complex that regulates transcription of β-catenin/WNT target genes and also serves as a coactivator of MYC. CDK8 gain synergizes with APC-driven WNT pathway activation. CDK8 inhibitors are under investigation in colorectal cancer.

3.3 Chromosome 18q Loss — TIER II (Oncogenic, Prognostic)
  Region: 18q12.1–18q23 (broad arm loss)
  Genes affected: SMAD4 (18q21.2), DCC (18q21.2), CABLES1, and others
  Interpretation: Loss of heterozygosity at chromosome 18q is the most common chromosomal alteration in colorectal cancer, occurring in approximately 60–70% of cases. Key tumor suppressor genes at 18q include SMAD4 (DPC4) and DCC (deleted in colorectal cancer). 18q LOH in the context of SMAD4 missense mutation (see 2.3) is consistent with biallelic SMAD4 inactivation (mutation + LOH). 18q loss is an established adverse prognostic marker in stage II/III CRC and contributes to the aggressive metastatic phenotype observed in stage IV disease. DCC loss may further impair netrin-mediated apoptotic signaling.

---

SECTION 4: GENE FUSIONS / STRUCTURAL REARRANGEMENTS

No pathogenic gene fusions or structural rearrangements were detected within the tested gene panel. NTRK1/2/3 fusions: not detected. ALK, RET, ROS1: not detected. RSPO2/3 fusions: not detected.

---

SECTION 5: MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) AND TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)

5.1 Microsatellite Instability Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Method: NGS-based analysis of 95 microsatellite loci (homopolymer and dinucleotide repeats)
  Result: 0 unstable loci of 95 examined (0.0% instability rate)
  Concordance: Consistent with IHC showing retained expression of all four MMR proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) as reported in prior pathology (colonoscopic biopsy)
  Interpretation: MSS status indicates an intact mismatch repair system and predicts lack of response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (anti-PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy, anti-CTLA-4 therapy). Per FDA labeling and NCCN guidelines, pembrolizumab and nivolumab are indicated for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC but NOT for MSS disease. This is a critical negative predictive biomarker for immunotherapy.

5.2 Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): LOW — 3.2 mutations/Mb
  Thresholds Applied: Low (<10 mut/Mb); High (≥10 mut/Mb per FDA TMB-H designation)
  Interpretation: Low TMB is consistent with MSS colorectal adenocarcinoma. TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) is associated with response to pembrolizumab per tumor-agnostic FDA approval (KEYNOTE-158); this patient does not meet that threshold. Low TMB in MSS CRC further supports limited immunotherapy benefit.

---

SECTION 6: EXTENDED RAS/RAF MUTATIONAL ANALYSIS (COLORECTAL-SPECIFIC REPORTING)

KRAS Codon-Level Analysis:
  Codon 12: p.G12D DETECTED (c.35G>A, VAF 42%) — see Section 1.1
  Codon 13: Wild-type
  Codon 59: Wild-type
  Codon 61: Wild-type
  Codon 117: Wild-type
  Codon 146: Wild-type

NRAS Mutational Analysis (Exons 2, 3, 4):
  Codon 12: Wild-type
  Codon 13: Wild-type
  Codon 59: Wild-type
  Codon 61: Wild-type
  Codon 117: Wild-type
  Codon 146: Wild-type
  NRAS Status: WILD-TYPE

BRAF Mutational Analysis (Exons 11 and 15):
  V600E (c.1799T>A): Not detected
  V600K: Not detected
  Other exon 11 mutations (G469, G464): Not detected
  BRAF Status: WILD-TYPE (non-V600E, non-class I)
  Interpretation: BRAF V600E-negative status excludes the aggressive BRAF-mutant subtype of CRC (which carries a very poor prognosis and may benefit from BRAF/MEK/EGFR combination inhibition). However, anti-EGFR therapy remains contraindicated due to KRAS G12D mutation.

HER2 (ERBB2) Assessment:
  Copy Number: 2.1 (no amplification)
  Immunohistochemistry correlation: IHC 1+ (per clinical records)
  HER2 Amplification Status: NOT AMPLIFIED
  Interpretation: HER2-directed therapies (trastuzumab/pertuzumab, trastuzumab deruxtecan) are indicated for HER2-amplified/overexpressed mCRC (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ with ISH amplification). This patient does not meet criteria. HER2 IHC 1+ is considered HER2-low in breast cancer paradigm but has no current actionable implication in CRC outside of clinical trials.

---

SECTION 7: GERMLINE FINDINGS AND HEREDITARY CANCER GENE ANALYSIS

Germline Sequencing Performed: Yes (paired peripheral blood leukocyte DNA)
Germline Variants Identified in Hereditary CRC / Lynch Syndrome Genes:
  MLH1: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified
  MSH2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified
  MSH6: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified
  PMS2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified
  APC (germline): No pathogenic variant identified (the APC p.R1450* detected in tumor DNA is confirmed somatic; not present in matched germline)
  MUTYH: No biallelic pathogenic variants identified (monoallelic variant of uncertain significance noted; see below)
  EPCAM: No pathogenic variant identified
  PTEN: No pathogenic variant identified
  BMPR1A, SMAD4 (germline): No pathogenic variant identified

Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS) — Germline:
  MUTYH p.G396D (c.1187G>A): Heterozygous carrier; this variant is a common pathogenic variant in biallelic MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP); heterozygous carriers have a modestly elevated CRC risk but monoallelic MUTYH variants are generally not considered causative of MAP. In the setting of MSS tumor without hypermutation, monoallelic MUTYH carrier status does not explain this patient's cancer. Genetic counseling referral recommended for family member testing if clinically indicated.

Summary: No evidence of Lynch syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP/AFAP), MUTYH-associated polyposis, Cowden syndrome, juvenile polyposis syndrome, or other hereditary CRC syndrome. Tumor appears to represent sporadic colorectal adenocarcinoma.

---

SECTION 8: PATHWAY INTEGRATION AND BIOLOGICAL SUMMARY

This tumor harbors a highly characteristic molecular profile of left-sided, sporadic, chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway colorectal adenocarcinoma:

  - WNT/β-catenin pathway: Constitutively activated via biallelic APC loss (p.R1450* + 18q-associated allelic loss)
  - RAS-MAPK pathway: Constitutively activated via KRAS G12D mutation
  - PI3K-AKT pathway: Activated via PIK3CA p.E545K (subclonal)
  - TGF-β/SMAD pathway: Inactivated via SMAD4 p.R361H + 18q LOH (biallelic loss)
  - p53 pathway: Inactivated via TP53 p.R248W dominant-negative/GOF mutation
  - MYC signaling: Amplified/overactivated via MYC amplification (8q24) + FBXW7 mutation (reduced MYC degradation)
  - Cell cycle regulation: Dysregulated via CDK8 gain, MYC amplification

The convergence of activation across RAS-MAPK, PI3K, WNT, and MYC pathways alongside inactivation of p53 and TGF-β tumor suppressor programs defines an aggressive oncogenomic landscape. The subclonal nature of PIK3CA E545K (VAF 19%) and FBXW7 R465H (VAF 14%) compared to clonal APC, TP53, and KRAS alterations (VAF 42–48%) is consistent with a clonal evolution model in which APC, TP53, and KRAS are founding events, with PIK3CA and FBXW7 mutations arising in later subclones, potentially associated with disease progression or metastatic evolution.

The combination of SMAD4 loss and PIK3CA co-mutation, in the context of RAS mutation, creates a particularly treatment-refractory genomic profile. Anti-EGFR therapy is definitively contraindicated (KRAS exon 2 mutant). Immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors is not indicated (MSS, low TMB). No currently approved targeted therapy exists for KRAS G12D in the CRC setting, though enrollment in a KRASG12D-directed clinical trial (e.g., MRTX1133 or combination strategies) should be explored at time of disease progression.

---

SECTION 9: CLINICAL TRIAL ELIGIBILITY CONSIDERATIONS (Informational)

Based on molecular profile identified:
  - KRAS G12D direct inhibitor trials (e.g., MRTX1133, BI-1823911 combinations): Potentially eligible
  - Pan-RAS/SOS1 inhibitor trials: Potentially eligible
  - PIK3CA inhibitor trials (subclonal finding; eligibility criteria may vary): Possible eligibility
  - SMAD4-loss enriched trials: Possible eligibility
  - MYC amplification/CDK8 inhibitor trials: Possible eligibility
  Note: Clinical trial eligibility should be confirmed against current enrollment criteria. Referral to a comprehensive cancer center with active Phase I/II program recommended at time of therapeutic need.

---

SECTION 10: QUALITY METRICS

Total Sequencing Reads: 412,000,000
On-Target Rate: 94.8%
Mean Coverage (target regions): 872x
Percent Bases >250x Coverage: 99.1%
Uniformity (% bases > 20% mean): 99.4%
Single Nucleotide Variant (SNV) Analytic Sensitivity at ≥5% VAF: >99%
SNV Specificity: >99.9%
Fusion RNA Detection: Adequate RNA input; all housekeeping controls passed
MSI Loci Covered: 95/95 (100%)
TMB Calculation: Based on non-synonymous coding mutations, excluding known germline variants, common SNPs, and low-confidence calls
Specimen Adequacy: ADEQUATE — all quality thresholds met

---

SECTION 11: LIMITATIONS

This assay detects somatic alterations in the 523 genes included in this panel and does not represent whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing. Variants in intronic regions, regulatory regions, and genes not included in the panel are not assessed. Large structural rearrangements involving novel non-panel genes may not be detected. Copy number analysis is based on comparative read-depth methodology and may not detect low-level mosaicism. This report reflects the molecular profile of the submitted specimen at the time of biopsy and may not represent the full heterogeneity of the tumor or metastatic deposits. Tumor evolutionary dynamics may result in divergence between primary and metastatic lesion molecular profiles. All variants should be interpreted in the full clinical context by a qualified physician.

---

ATTENDING MOLECULAR PATHOLOGIST: [SIGNATURE ON FILE]
Molecular Pathology, Genomics Division
Board-Certified: Anatomic Pathology / Molecular Genetic Pathology

REPORT STATUS: FINAL
CAP/CLIA Accredited Laboratory
